card_title: How much PayPal actually costs you on a $20K supplier payment

videos.video_title: What a $20K PayPal supplier payment actually costs

variants.vo: Pattern we see across operators. SMEs start out paying suppliers on PayPal because it's the rail they already use. Works fine on a 300 dollar sample. Then the orders grow. By the time someone hits 20 grand, the maths stops working. PayPal's cross-border B2B fee is 4.4 percent. On 20K that's 880 dollars. Then there's another 3.5 percent in the FX rate. That's 700 more. And the supplier quietly pads the price 4 to 5 percent because they hate receiving PayPal. So the real cost lands closer to 22 and a half thousand on a 20K order. WorldFirst was built for cross-border business, not personal payments. Bio.

Quick maths on a 20 grand PayPal payment to a Chinese supplier. Cross-border transaction fee, 4.4 percent. That's 880 dollars. FX margin to CNY, around 3.5 percent. Another 700. Supplier usually adds 4 to 5 percent on the PI to cover their receiving fee. Call that 800 more. Add it up. 2,380 dollars on a 20K order. That's almost 12 percent. Sent through a business rail at near-interbank, the same payment costs under 100. WorldFirst was built for cross-border business, not personal payments. Bio.

Everyone says PayPal is the easy option for paying suppliers. Truth is, PayPal was built for buying a hoodie online, not for sending 20 grand to a factory. The 4.4 percent fee makes sense on a 200 dollar order. On 20K it's 880 dollars. The 3.5 percent FX margin is invisible. The supplier markup, because most Chinese suppliers hate receiving PayPal, that's another 800 sitting in the PI quietly. Same product. Same brand. Different math at scale. Personal tools were not built for business volume. WorldFirst was built for cross-border business, not personal payments. Bio.

Pattern we see across operators. First sample order goes out on PayPal. 200 bucks, fee is 9 dollars, nobody notices. Two years later, same app, same supplier, but the orders are 20 grand. The fee is now 880 dollars. Same UI. Same brand. Same screen. The percentage didn't change. The dollar amount did. And the app never warned anyone they'd outgrown it. The volume crept up. The cost crept up with it. Most operators don't see it until their accountant flags it. WorldFirst was built for cross-border business, not personal payments. Bio.

PayPal on 200 dollars. Fee is around 9 bucks. That's 4.5 percent but it feels fine because the absolute is small. Now PayPal on 20 grand. Same 4.4 percent. The fee is 880 dollars. Plus FX margin. Plus supplier markup. The percentage didn't change. The dollar amount got loud. If you wouldn't hand a bank 880 bucks to wire 20K, don't hand an app 880 bucks to wire 20K. WorldFirst quotes near-interbank because we're built for business, not personal. Bio.

Everyone says PayPal is cheap. They're right, on 200 dollars. They're not right on 20,000. Same product, same fee structure, completely different reality. At 200 the fixed bit absorbs the pain. At 20K the percentage takes over and 880 dollars walks out the door. The app didn't change. The volume did. Most operators don't notice because the UI never changes. WorldFirst was built for cross-border business, not personal payments. Bio.

card_title: Credit cards for international supplier payments: the gap

videos.video_title: Why your supplier said no to the card

variants.vo: Pattern we see. Operators try a credit card for Chinese suppliers about once. Then they switch. Card acceptance among Chinese suppliers sits at roughly 3 to 5 percent. The other 95 run on Alipay and WeChat Pay domestically. And the few who do accept add a 2 to 4 percent surcharge to cover their own processing cost. Then there's the 180-day chargeback window on goods that ship in 30. From the supplier's angle, that isn't payment. That's a loan with a return policy. So they decline. Quietly. Politely. T/T only please. WorldFirst pays Chinese suppliers like they're local. Bio.

The maths on why Chinese suppliers refuse cards. Card acceptance rate among Chinese suppliers, 3 to 5 percent. Processing fee they'd absorb, 2.95 to 2.99 percent. Chargeback window, 180 days, on goods that shipped in 30. So a 10K order has a 2.99 percent fee. That's roughly 300 dollars they pay. Plus six months of dispute exposure on something already delivered. They'd rather lose the order. The category was built for retail, not factory orders. WorldFirst pays Chinese suppliers like they're local. Bio.

Everyone says just use a business card to pay the factory. Truth is, the factory probably can't accept it, and wouldn't if they could. Cards were built for retail, where the buyer is the consumer and chargebacks protect them on a 50 dollar item. Factory orders run on T/T because the supplier needs the money to actually clear, not sit in dispute purgatory for six months. Cards aren't broken. They were built for a different category. WorldFirst pays Chinese suppliers like they're local. Bio.

card_title: Personal vs business FX rate: the spread

videos.video_title: The rate you see isn't the rate that exists

variants.vo: Pattern we see across our books. The foreign exchange spread between personal cross-border apps and business cross-border tools is 1.5 to 2 percent. Same currency pair. Same minute. Two different prices. The interbank rate, the one Google shows you, that's what banks use among themselves. Personal wallets quote a retail rate with 2 to 4 percent already baked in. It looks like a rate change. It's a rate tier. Operators refresh, see the same gap, and assume their timing is bad. The timing isn't bad. The tier is. WorldFirst quotes near-interbank because we're business, not personal. Bio.

Quick maths on the rate you actually get. Google shows you the interbank rate. That's what banks pay each other. On personal wallets, the rate you see has 2 to 4 percent built in. Run a 20K supplier wire at 2 percent margin, that's 400 dollars you didn't see. On a business rail at 0.5 percent, that's 100. The currency moved the same direction. The rate just had less baked into it. WorldFirst quotes near-interbank because we're business, not personal. Bio.

Everyone says check the rate on Google before you send. Truth is, that rate doesn't exist for your wallet. The interbank rate is what banks use among each other. Personal accounts see a retail rate with 2 to 4 percent margin already in it. It's not a rate change. It's a rate tier. Personal accounts pay personal rates. Business accounts get quoted closer to interbank. Same currency pair, same minute, two prices. WorldFirst quotes near-interbank because we're business, not personal. Bio.

videos.video_title: Personal vs business FX: the same currency, two prices

variants.vo: Pattern we see. Two operators send CNY to the same supplier on the same Tuesday. One from a personal account, one from a business account. Same provider on the front. Different rate on the back. Personal might quote 0.243. Business might quote 0.247. Same currency pair. Same minute. Tiny gap. On a 20K supplier wire, that gap is 800 dollars. Nobody negotiated. They just had different account types. The personal tier sees retail spread. Business sees something closer to institutional. WorldFirst quotes near-interbank because we're business, not personal. Bio.

Quick maths on the personal versus business foreign exchange gap. Personal tier on most platforms, 1.5 to 2 percent off interbank. Business tier, 0.3 to 1 percent. On a 20K supplier wire, that's 300 dollars of spread difference. Run it weekly, that's 1,200 dollars a month, 14 grand a year, never appearing on a line item. It's just inside the rate. The fix isn't switching providers. It's switching tiers. WorldFirst quotes near-interbank because we're business, not personal. Bio.

Everyone says Wise gives you the best rate. They do, on personal transfers. Truth is, the rate on a personal account and a business account is never the same, even on the same app. Personal tier runs at retail spread. Business tier runs at institutional spread. The gap is 1 to 2 percentage points and it shows up on every single transfer. On 20K weekly, that's 1,200 a month gone. Quietly. WorldFirst quotes near-interbank because we're business, not personal. Bio.

card_title: When CAN you pay a Chinese supplier by card?

videos.video_title: When CAN you pay a Chinese supplier by card?

variants.vo: Pattern we see. Cards work for Chinese suppliers in exactly three scenarios. One, samples and first orders under 1,000 dollars, where the 2.95 percent fee is small and chargeback protects you if the samples are bad. Two, Alibaba Trade Assurance orders up to 12K, where Alibaba escrows the dispute risk. Three, through a sourcing agent, who takes the card on their side and pays the factory in CNY for a 3 to 5 percent bridge fee. Outside those three, operators end up on T/T or a business rail. Cards have a use. Most operators just over-extend them. WorldFirst pays Chinese suppliers like they're local. Bio.

Three scenarios where cards actually work for Chinese suppliers. One, samples and first orders under 1,000 dollars. Card fee is 2.95 percent, low absolute cost, plus 180 days of chargeback protection if the samples are bad. Two, Alibaba Trade Assurance orders capped at 12,000 per order. Alibaba holds the dispute risk. Three, sourcing agents. Agent takes the card, settles to the factory in CNY, charges 3 to 5 percent for the bridge. Outside those three, it's T/T or a business rail. WorldFirst pays Chinese suppliers like they're local. Bio.

Everyone says cards never work for Chinese suppliers. Truth is, they work in three scenarios. Samples under a grand, where the 2.95 percent fee is small and chargeback covers you. Alibaba Trade Assurance, capped at 12K, where Alibaba escrows the risk. And via a sourcing agent who bridges the card to CNY for a 3 to 5 percent fee. The mistake operators make is thinking cards scale. They don't. They're a sample tool. Production runs on T/T. WorldFirst pays Chinese suppliers like they're local. Bio.

Pattern we see. Operators try to put 14 or 15 grand on their card through Alibaba. Declined. They call their bank. Card is fine. Then they read the Trade Assurance terms. Card payments are capped at 12,000 dollars per order. That ceiling isn't arbitrary. Above 12K, the chargeback risk on a single order is too large for Alibaba to escrow against a factory. So they push you to T/T. 12 grand is roughly where sample buying becomes production buying. Cards live below the wall. Production lives above it. Same factory. Different rail. WorldFirst pays Chinese suppliers like they're local. Bio.

Alibaba's card payment cap is 12,000 dollars per order. Below that, card works with Trade Assurance, fee around 2.95 percent, escrow protection. Above 12K, rejected at checkout. The reason is risk. Alibaba can't escrow a 50K chargeback against a single supplier order. So they push you to T/T or a business rail. The 12K line is where sample buying ends and production buying starts. WorldFirst handles every payment above that wall, in CNY, direct to your supplier. Bio.

Everyone says cards are the easy way to pay Alibaba. Truth is, there's a hard 12 grand wall on card payments per order. Below it, fine, Trade Assurance escrows the risk. Above it, rejected. That ceiling isn't a glitch. It's the line between sample buying and production buying. Cards were never built to back a 50K factory order. Production runs on T/T or a business rail. WorldFirst pays Chinese suppliers like they're local. Bio.
